section.title: Jehoshaphat's Victory and Death
section.sectionRationale: A vast coalition marches against Judah, and Jehoshaphat, powerless before them, turns the whole nation to seek the LORD in prayer and fasting. The prophet Jahaziel announces that the battle belongs to God, and as Judah goes out singing praise the enemy destroys itself. The chapter closes with a summary of Jehoshaphat's good but incomplete reign and a rebuke for allying with wicked Ahaziah of Israel.
section.thesis: When the king and the whole assembly confess their powerlessness and fix their eyes on the LORD, God fights the battle for them, so that praise, not the sword, becomes the weapon of victory.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the threat and the fast, verses 1 to 4; Jehoshaphat's prayer, verses 5 to 13; Jahaziel's prophecy and the people's worship, verses 14 to 19; the singers march out and the enemy destroys itself, verses 20 to 30; and a summary of Jehoshaphat's reign, including the wrecked alliance with Ahaziah, verses 31 to 37.

units.label: War against Jehoshaphat
units.note: a vast army coming against you

verses.text: After this, the Moabites and Ammonites, together with some of the Meunites, came to make war against Jehoshaphat.
Then some men came and told Jehoshaphat, "A vast army is coming against you from Edom, from beyond the Sea, they are already in Hazazon-tamar" (that is, En-gedi).
Jehoshaphat was alarmed and set his face to {{darash|seek}} the LORD. And he proclaimed a fast throughout Judah.
So the people of Judah gathered to seek the LORD, and indeed, they came from all the cities of Judah to seek Him.
Then Jehoshaphat stood in the assembly of Judah and Jerusalem in the house of the LORD in front of the new courtyard

and said, "O LORD, God of our fathers, are You not the God who is in heaven, and do You not rule over all the kingdoms of the nations? Power and might are in Your hand, and no one can stand against You.
Our God, did You not drive out the inhabitants of this land before Your people Israel and give it forever to the descendants of Abraham Your friend?
They have lived in the land and have built in it a sanctuary for Your Name, saying,

'If disaster comes upon us, whether sword or judgment, plague or famine, we will stand before this temple and before You, for Your Name is in this temple. We will cry out to You in our distress, and You will hear us and save us.'
And now, here are the men of Ammon, Moab, and Mount Seir, whom You did not let Israel invade when they came out of the land of Egypt. So Israel turned away from them and did not destroy them.
See how they are repaying us by coming to drive us out of the possession that You gave us as an inheritance.
Our God, will You not judge them? For we are powerless before this vast army that comes against us. We do not know what to do, but our eyes are upon You."
Meanwhile all the men of Judah, with their wives and children and little ones, were standing before the LORD.

units.label: The prophecy of Jahaziel
units.note: the battle does not belong to you

verses.text: Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jahaziel son of Zechariah, the son of Benaiah, the son of Jeiel, the son of Mattaniah, a Levite from Asaph's descendants, as he stood in the midst of the assembly.

And he said, "Listen, all you people of Judah and Jerusalem! Listen, King Jehoshaphat! This is what the LORD says: 'Do not be afraid or discouraged because of this vast army, for the battle does not belong to you, but to God.
Tomorrow you are to march down against them. You will see them coming up the Ascent of Ziz, and you will find them at the end of the valley facing the Wilderness of Jeruel.

You need not fight this battle. Take up your positions, stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD on your behalf, O Judah and Jerusalem. Do not be afraid or discouraged. Go out and face them tomorrow, for the LORD is with you.'"
Then Jehoshaphat bowed facedown, and all the people of Judah and Jerusalem fell down before the LORD to worship Him.
And the Levites from the Kohathites and Korahites stood up to praise the LORD, the God of Israel, shouting in a very loud voice.

Early in the morning they got up and left for the Wilderness of Tekoa. As they set out, Jehoshaphat stood up and said, "Hear me, O people of Judah and Jerusalem. {{aman|Believe}} in the LORD your God, and you will be upheld; believe in His prophets, and you will succeed."

Then Jehoshaphat consulted with the people and appointed those who would sing to the LORD and praise the splendor of His holiness. As they went out before the army, they were singing: "Give thanks to the LORD, for His loving devotion endures forever."
The moment they began their shouts and praises, the LORD set ambushes against the men of Ammon, Moab, and Mount Seir who had come against Judah, and they were defeated.
The Ammonites and Moabites rose up against the inhabitants of Mount Seir, devoting them to destruction. And when they had finished off the inhabitants of Seir, they helped to destroy one another.
When the men of Judah came to a place overlooking the wilderness, they looked for the vast army, but there were only corpses lying on the ground; no one had escaped.

Then Jehoshaphat and his people went to carry off the plunder, and they found on the bodies an abundance of goods and valuables, more than they could carry away. They were gathering the plunder for three days because there was so much.

units.label: The joyful return and Jehoshaphat's summary
units.note: his God had given him rest

verses.text: On the fourth day they assembled in the Valley of Beracah, where they blessed the LORD. Therefore that place is called the Valley of Beracah to this day.
Then all the men of Judah and Jerusalem, with Jehoshaphat at their head, returned joyfully to Jerusalem, for the LORD had made them rejoice over their enemies.
So they entered Jerusalem and went into the house of the LORD with harps, lyres, and trumpets.
And the fear of God came upon all the kingdoms of the lands when they heard that the LORD had fought against the enemies of Israel.
Then Jehoshaphat's kingdom was at peace, for his God had given him rest on every side.
So Jehoshaphat reigned over Judah. He was thirty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem twenty-five years. His mother's name was Azubah daughter of Shilhi.
And Jehoshaphat walked in the way of his father Asa and did not turn away from it; he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD.
The high places, however, were not removed; the people had not yet set their hearts on the God of their fathers.
As for the rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, from beginning to end, they are indeed written in the Chronicles of Jehu son of Hanani, which are recorded in the Book of the Kings of Israel.
Later, Jehoshaphat king of Judah made an alliance with Ahaziah king of Israel, who acted wickedly.
They agreed to make ships to go to Tarshish, and these were built in Ezion-geber.

Then Eliezer son of Dodavahu of Mareshah prophesied against Jehoshaphat, saying, "Because you have allied yourself with Ahaziah, the LORD has destroyed your works." So the ships were wrecked and were unable to sail to Tarshish.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, or resort to. Jehoshaphat sets his face to darash the LORD; the whole nation follows him in seeking God through fasting and prayer rather than seeking safety through weapons or alliances first.

aman.gloss: To believe, trust, be established or confirmed. The same root gives us amen. Jehoshaphat tells the people to believe in the LORD and you will be upheld, literally established, the same word used of a house or throne standing firm because it rests on something trustworthy.

basic.context: A massive coalition of Moabites, Ammonites, and Meunites marches against Judah. Jehoshaphat, terrified and with no military answer, calls the whole nation to fast and pray, then stands in the temple courtyard and prays aloud, ending with the honest confession, we do not know what to do, but our eyes are on You. God answers through the Levite Jahaziel: the battle is not yours but God's. The next day Judah marches out with singers in front of the army, and the LORD causes the invading armies to turn on each other and destroy themselves. Judah gathers plunder for three days and returns home praising God. The chapter ends with a summary of Jehoshaphat's generally faithful reign, and a rebuke for later allying with the wicked king Ahaziah of Israel, an alliance God wrecks.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, not a promise that God will always destroy our enemies without our involvement. The abiding pattern is that God delights to fight for His people when they honestly confess their weakness, seek Him first, and trust His word through His appointed messengers. The chapter does not prescribe passivity in every crisis, but it does prescribe seeking the LORD before, not instead of, taking any action.
basic.originalLanguages: Jehoshaphat sets his face to darash, seek, the LORD, gathering the whole nation to seek Him together. His climactic word is not a strategy but an honest confession of powerlessness. Then he tells the people to aman, believe or be established, in the LORD and in His prophets, the same root behind the word amen, showing that real security rests on trusting what God has said, not on military strength.
basic.typology: Judah's singers marching ahead of the army, and God winning the battle without a sword being raised by Judah's own hand, previews a greater pattern: salvation accomplished for God's people rather than by them, fulfilled fully at the cross where Christ alone wins the decisive victory over sin and death while His people simply stand, believe, and receive it.
basic.christ: Jehoshaphat's prayer, we do not know what to do, but our eyes are on You, models the posture every believer takes toward Christ: honest about our own powerlessness, and fixed in hope on the One who fights for us. Just as Judah's praise went out ahead of the battle and the victory was already secured before a blow was struck, so the gospel proclaims a victory Christ has already won, and calls us simply to believe and give thanks.
basic.god: God is the true warrior for His people; He is not distant from their fear but meets it directly through His prophetic word, promising presence, not just eventual rescue, for the LORD is with you. He delights to be sought, He answers honest prayer, and He turns the schemes of the proud coalition into their own undoing, showing that no power arrayed against His people can ultimately prevail.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 14:14 shows the LORD fighting for Israel at the Red Sea while the people only need to stand still. Isaiah 7:9 links believing to being established, the same wordplay as verse 20. Romans 8:31 asks, if God is for us, who can be against us. 2 Corinthians 6:14 warns against unequal alliances, echoed in Jehoshaphat's later partnership with wicked Ahaziah.
basic.oneStory: When the king and the nation confess they have no answer of their own and fix their eyes on the LORD, God fights the battle and Judah only needs to praise Him. This is the gospel pattern in miniature: God secures the victory His people could never win themselves, and calls them to trust, worship, and receive it, while any alliance with what is wicked, however tempting, still wrecks what praise had built.

context.literary: The chapter moves from crisis, to corporate prayer, to prophetic assurance, to worship preceding battle, to a victory won without swords, to joyful return, closing with a summary formula and a cautionary coda about a foolish alliance. The structure itself preaches: worship comes before, not after, the outcome is known.
context.historical: Moab, Ammon, and the hill country of Seir lay east and south of Judah; such coalitions of smaller regional powers against Judah were a recurring threat during the divided monarchy. The route through En-gedi along the Dead Sea and the ascent of Ziz reflects real regional geography known to the original audience.
context.canonical: This is one of the clearest Old Testament pictures of God fighting for His people apart from their own military effort, standing alongside the Red Sea crossing and Gideon's three hundred, and anticipating the New Testament's repeated call to stand firm and see the salvation already accomplished in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that when the king and the whole assembly of Judah earnestly and humbly sought the LORD in crisis, God answered decisively, granting a victory won entirely by His own hand, while also recording, honestly, that even this same king later erred by allying with a wicked ally.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical deliverance; it is not a formula guaranteeing that fasting and singing will always prevent battle or loss. What endures is the pattern of honest, corporate dependence on God in crisis, and the warning that unequal alliances with those who do evil still bring real loss even to an otherwise faithful king.

debates.question: Why does the chronicler include the failed alliance with Ahaziah right after such a triumphant chapter?

views.label: A sobering coda that guards against triumphalism
views.case: By placing Jehoshaphat's lapse immediately after his greatest victory, the chronicler warns readers that no past faithfulness exempts a leader from present temptation, and that even a king who prayed so well can still choose a wrong ally.

views.label: A consistent pattern across Jehoshaphat's reign
views.case: 2 Chronicles 18 already shows Jehoshaphat allying with wicked Ahab of Israel; this closing note shows the same recurring weakness, unequal alliance with the northern kingdom's compromised royal house, bookending his reign as a whole.

debates.bearing: Either way, the juxtaposition teaches that dependence on God in crisis and susceptibility to wrong alliances can coexist in the same faithful leader, a sober word for any believer who has known great answered prayer.

study.originalLanguages: Jehoshaphat sets his face to darash, seek, the LORD, gathering all Judah to seek Him together rather than seeking safety by their own strength first. His climactic confession is not a strategy but an honest admission of powerlessness. Jehoshaphat then calls the people to aman, believe or be established, in the LORD and in His prophets, the same root behind amen, tying real security to trusting what God has spoken rather than to Judah's own military resources.

typology.type: Singers marching ahead of the army into a battle already won
typology.fulfillment: Christ's finished victory over sin and death, received by faith and praise rather than earned by our own struggle, 1 Corinthians 15:57.

typology.type: The people who could only stand and see the salvation of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Believers who stand firm in a salvation Christ alone has accomplished, Ephesians 6:13-14.

study.christ: Jehoshaphat's confession, we do not know what to do, but our eyes are on You, is the posture of everyone who comes to Christ empty handed. The LORD's answer through Jahaziel, the battle does not belong to you, but to God, anticipates the gospel's central claim: salvation is accomplished by God for His people, not achieved by their own strength. Judah's singers marching ahead of an army into a fight already decided pictures the church's calling to proclaim a victory Christ has already won at the cross and the empty tomb, standing firm and giving thanks rather than striving to win what has already been secured.
study.god: God meets honest fear with a specific, personal word through His prophet rather than mere silence, and He is faithful to fight for those who seek Him first. He is also a God who lets consequences fall even on a generally faithful king when he binds himself to wickedness, showing that His holiness governs every alliance His people make, not only their moments of crisis.

crossReferences.note: The LORD fights for Israel at the sea while the people are told only to stand firm and see His salvation, the same pattern as Jehoshaphat's battle.
If you do not stand firm in your faith, you will not stand at all, a wordplay on the same root behind believe in 2 Chronicles 20:20.
If God is for us, who can be against us, the New Testament's confidence echoing Jehoshaphat's trust.
Do not be unequally yoked, a principle Jehoshaphat violates in his alliance with Ahaziah at the chapter's close.

study.oneStory: Jehoshaphat's honest prayer of powerlessness and the LORD's answer, the battle is not yours but God's, is a vivid rehearsal of the whole gospel story: God's people cannot save themselves, but the LORD fights and wins on their behalf, and calls them to believe, worship, and give thanks. The chapter's sober close, a wrecked alliance with a wicked king, reminds every reader that trusting God in crisis and compromising with evil in comfort can exist side by side, and points forward to the greater Son of David who never faltered in either.

sermon.title: The Secret of True Power
sermon.why: Begg expounds 2 Chronicles 20:1-37, showing how Jehoshaphat's honest confession of weakness and his call to seek and trust the LORD becomes the true secret of strength for God's people in crisis, matching this movement's passage exactly.

quiz.q: How did Jehoshaphat respond when he learned a vast army was marching against Judah?

options.text: He was alarmed, but set his face to seek the LORD and called the whole nation to fast and pray.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 show fear driving him to seek God corporately, not to panic or immediate battle plans.

options.text: He immediately gathered the army and marched out to meet the enemy.
options.note: Judah does eventually march out, but only after seeking the LORD first and receiving a prophetic word; the initial response was prayer and fasting.

options.text: He ignored the threat since Judah had a large enough army.
options.note: Jehoshaphat explicitly confesses powerlessness rather than confidence in Judah's own strength.

options.text: He surrendered to the coalition without resistance.
options.note: Jehoshaphat sought the LORD and ultimately saw victory; he never surrendered.

quiz.q: What was the heart of Jehoshaphat's public prayer in the temple courtyard?

options.text: A confession of Judah's powerlessness and total dependence, ending, we do not know what to do, but our eyes are upon You.
options.note: Verse 12 is the prayer's climactic confession of dependence on God alone.

options.text: A request that God bless Judah's own battle strategy.
options.note: The prayer does ask God to act, but the emphasis is helplessness and dependence, not endorsing a human strategy.

options.text: A demand that God punish Judah's enemies immediately.
options.note: The prayer does ask God to judge the enemy, but its center is confessed weakness and trust, not merely a demand for vengeance.

options.text: A boast about Judah's past military victories.
options.note: The prayer recalls God's past faithfulness, not Judah's own achievements, and centers on present helplessness.

quiz.q: What did the prophet Jahaziel announce to the assembly?

options.text: That the battle belonged to God, not to Judah, and they need only take their positions and see the LORD's salvation.
options.note: Verses 15 and 17 give this exact prophetic assurance.

options.text: That Judah must first raise a much larger army before going out.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly says Judah need not fight this battle at all, the opposite of calling for a larger army.

options.text: That God would not help unless Judah paid a heavy tribute.
options.note: No tribute or payment is mentioned; the prophecy promises free deliverance in response to seeking the LORD.

options.text: That Jerusalem would fall regardless of what they did.
options.note: The prophecy promises victory and salvation, not defeat.

quiz.q: How did the LORD actually defeat the invading coalition?

options.text: He set ambushes so the invading armies turned on and destroyed one another while Judah's singers were still praising Him.
options.note: Verses 22 to 24 describe the enemy's self destruction as Judah's army had not even engaged them.

options.text: Judah's army won a hard fought conventional battle.
options.note: Judah never actually fought; the enemy destroyed itself before Judah's forces arrived at the battlefield.

options.text: A drought forced the invading armies to retreat.
options.note: The text describes ambushes and mutual destruction among the enemy armies, not a retreat caused by drought.

options.text: The invading armies were never actually defeated.
options.note: Verse 24 explicitly states there were only corpses on the ground and no one had escaped.

quiz.q: What sobering event closes out the chapter after Jehoshaphat's summary?

options.text: Jehoshaphat allied with wicked King Ahaziah of Israel to build ships, and a prophet announced the LORD had destroyed the venture.
options.note: Verses 35 to 37 record this failed alliance and its prophetic rebuke.

options.text: Jehoshaphat completely removed all the high places from Judah.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly states the high places were not removed, the opposite of this claim.

options.text: Jehoshaphat was punished with a fatal illness for pride.
options.note: The specific rebuke recorded here concerns his alliance with Ahaziah and the wrecked ships, not an illness.

options.text: No further events are recorded after the victory celebration.
options.note: Verses 31 to 37 record a detailed summary and the failed alliance with Ahaziah.

questions: Jehoshaphat's prayer ends with an honest confession, we do not know what to do, but our eyes are on You. Where in your life right now do you need to trade a plan you cannot make work for that same honest dependence on God?
Judah's singers marched out ahead of the army into a battle already decided. How does knowing that Christ has already won the decisive victory change the way you face a fear or a fight this week?
The whole nation, including wives and children, gathered to seek the LORD together. Who could you invite into seeking God with you the next time you face something beyond your own strength?

Even after such a clear answer to prayer, Jehoshaphat later allied himself with a wicked king and lost what he had built. Where might present comfort be tempting you toward a partnership or compromise you would never choose in a moment of crisis?

The people were told to believe in the LORD and be established, and to believe His prophets and succeed. What promise of God's word do you need to actively trust today rather than merely agree with in theory?

translationNotes.note: The BSB renders an em dash as whether sword or judgment; here rendered with a comma, following the sense that disaster in view could be war or divine judgment, plague, or famine.
Believe and be upheld and believe... and succeed both translate forms of the Hebrew root aman, the same root behind amen, linking trust in God to being established or made secure.

Devoting them to destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem, the practice of giving persons or things over to the LORD by destroying them, here carried out by the invading coalition against each other rather than by Judah.
The BSB text here follows the reading valuables rather than clothing found in some manuscripts and the Vulgate; either way the point is an overwhelming abundance of plunder.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 20:1-37. Two em dashes in the source, verses 9 and 25, were converted to commas per house style.
